SUMMARY:
Single-cell omics technologies have revolutionized molecular profiling by providing high-resolution insights into cellular heterogeneity and complexity. Single-cell analysis driven by high-throughput sequencing and mass spectrometry provides high-resolution insights into genomes, transcriptomes, proteomes, and epigenetics. G&T-seq: parallel sequencing of single-cell genomes and transcriptomes. A highly scalable method for joint whole-genome sequencing and gene-expression profiling of single cells. Simultaneous profiling of 3D genome structure and DNA methylation in single human cells. Integrative single-cell analysis of transcriptome, DNA methylome and chromatin accessibility in mouse oocytes. An ultra high-throughput method for single-cell joint analysis of open chromatin and transcriptome. scNMT-seq enables joint profiling of chromatin accessibility DNA methylation and transcription in single cells. Linking genome structures to functions by simultaneous single-cell Hi-C and RNA-seq.

Abstract
--------

Single-cell omics technologies have revolutionized molecular profiling by providing high-resolution insights into cellular heterogeneity and complexity. Traditional bulk omics approaches average signals from heterogeneous cell populations, thereby obscuring important cellular nuances. Single-cell omics studies enable the analysis of individual cells and reveal diverse cell types, dynamic cellular states, and rare cell populations. These techniques offer unprecedented resolution and sensitivity, enabling researchers to unravel the molecular landscape of individual cells. Furthermore, the integration of multimodal omics data within a single cell provides a comprehensive and holistic view of cellular processes. By combining multiple omics dimensions, multimodal omics approaches can facilitate the elucidation of complex cellular interactions, regulatory networks, and molecular mechanisms. This integrative approach enhances our understanding of cellular systems, from development to disease. This review provides an overview of the recent advances in single-cell and multimodal omics for high-resolution molecular profiling. We discuss the principles and methodologies for representatives of each omics method, highlighting the strengths and limitations of the different techniques. In addition, we present case studies demonstrating the applications of single-cell and multimodal omics in various fields, including developmental biology, neurobiology, cancer research, immunology, and precision medicine.

Introduction
------------

Single-cell omics techniques have transformed biological research, offering unprecedented insights into cellular intricacies. Conventional bulk sequencing methods have a limited ability to uncover heterogeneity and complexity within a population of cells, as they provide averaged data. In contrast, single-cell sequencing enables the characterization of individual cells, revealing diverse cell types, dynamic cellular states, and rare cell populations that were concealed within the ensemble of bulk measurements.

Single-cell analysis driven by high-throughput sequencing and mass spectrometry provides high-resolution insights into genomes, transcriptomes, proteomes, and epigenetics. This approach uncovers hidden complexities in cellular landscapes, providing novel insights into cellular development, diseases, and cellular mechanisms.

However, biological systems are complex and driven by interactions between omics layers. In recognition of this, the emerging field of single-cell multimodal omics has emerged to integrate information across diverse molecular dimensions within a single cell. This approach provides a holistic view of processes, illuminating the interconnected networks that shape cell behavior.

Multimodal omics data enable the identification of causal relationships between layers, revealing how genetics affect gene expression, epigenetics, proteins, and metabolites. This integrative approach is particularly useful for dissecting complex diseases. Understanding the molecular mechanisms underlying disease pathogenesis requires a multidimensional perspective.

In this review, we discuss the key principles and technical considerations underlying single-cell omics and multimodal omics. We explore the technical principles, experimental workflows, and biological insights gained from these approaches. Additionally, we highlight the challenges and opportunities in the field, discuss emerging technologies, and provide insights into the future directions of single-cell and multimodal omics research.

Single-cell isolation and barcoding
-----------------------------------

Because single-cell sequencing technologies aim to understand and profile each cell according to its characteristics, cells must be separated and isolated from cell samples or whole tissues before analysis. Magnetic-activated cell sorting (MACS)[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR1 "Miltenyi, S., Muller, W., Weichel, W. & Radbruch, A. High gradient magnetic cell separation with MACS. Cytometry 11, 231–238 (1990)."), fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS)[2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR2 "Basu, S., Campbell, H. M., Dittel, B. N., Ray, A. Purification of specific cell population by fluorescence activated cell sorting (FACS). J.Vis.Exp. 1546 
                  https://doi.org/10.3791/1546
                  
                 (2010)."), and various microfluidic technologies are often employed for high-throughput studies. These technologies allow the isolation and analysis of large populations of cells in a more efficient and automated manner, enabling high-throughput experiments. FACS can simultaneously analyze cells according to size, granularity, and fluorescence, allowing multiparameter analysis[2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR2 "Basu, S., Campbell, H. M., Dittel, B. N., Ray, A. Purification of specific cell population by fluorescence activated cell sorting (FACS). J.Vis.Exp. 1546 
                  https://doi.org/10.3791/1546
                  
                 (2010)."). Although FACS has become a prominent single-cell isolation method because of its ability to analyze multiple parameters simultaneously and its high specificity, it has certain technical limitations. For example, FACS requires sufficient cell density for effective analysis and may face challenges in isolating single cells from low-density populations. Rapid flow and fluorescence exposure during FACS can affect cell viability, potentially resulting in separation failure.

Microfluidic devices have revolutionized single-cell analysis by enabling the high-throughput processing of tens of thousands of single cells. These devices consist of microfluidic channels and reaction chambers controlled by valves, allowing for precise manipulation and isolation of droplets within microfluidic chips, where each droplet encapsulates a single cell, enabling the parallel processing of numerous cells. Another technology utilizes devices with nanowells that provide individual compartments for single cells. Microfluidic devices offer significant advantages, including increased throughput, reduced cost per cell, and the ability to scale down reaction volumes to the nanoliter or picoliter scale. This reduction in reaction volume minimizes reagent consumption and can shift the main cost barrier from library preparation to sequencing. Consequently, researchers can generate larger datasets while optimizing costs[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR3 "Autebert, J. et al. Microfluidic: an innovative tool for efficient cell sorting. Methods 57, 297–307 (2012).").

Cell barcoding is a crucial step in a single-cell sequencing workflow, allowing libraries from multiple individual cells to be sequenced together in a single pool. This enables the efficient sequencing of many cells while preserving their identity for downstream analysis. In plate-based techniques, the cell barcode is typically added to the final PCR step before sequencing, in which sequencing libraries from different cells are combined. Microfluidics-based barcoding methods offer the advantage of incorporating cell barcodes into the protocol earlier, often allowing the entire pool of libraries to be processed in a single tube. This early incorporation of barcodes reduces the number of handling steps and the potential sample loss[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR4 "Kim, I. S. Single-cell molecular barcoding to decode multimodal information defining cell states. Mol. Cells 46, 74–85 (2023).").

Cell barcoding techniques offer a powerful means to barcode and sequence individual cells, enabling the deconvolution of sequence data into files that represent each cell. These methods facilitate high-throughput single-cell sequencing while preserving cell identity and enabling downstream analysis.

Objectives of single-cell mono-omics
------------------------------------

### Genome

Single-cell genomics provides a new perspective on biological functions through the study of genetic variants at the individual cell level. However, the small amount of DNA obtained from a single cell (typically at the picogram level) poses challenges for amplification and analysis. Genomic research has progressed rapidly in recent decades with the development of amplification methods. Whole-genome amplification (WGA) technologies have been developed to amplify fragments of the entire genome of a single cell while minimizing amplification errors and avoiding contamination.

One commonly used technique is degenerate oligonucleotide-primed (DOP)-PCR, which is a specialized PCR method for WGA. DOP-PCR utilizes primers with random sequences to bind to various sites in the genome, followed by a second amplification stage in which fragments tagged by a specific sequence are selectively amplified[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR5 "Cheung, V. G. & Nelson, S. F. Whole genome amplification using a degenerate oligonucleotide primer allows hundreds of genotypes to be performed on less than one nanogram of genomic DNA. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. USA 93, 14676–14679 (1996)."). Although DOP-PCR allows amplification from low amounts of starting materials, it can result in low genome coverage owing to site-specific preferential amplification. Multiple displacement amplification (MDA) amplifies DNA isothermally using φ29 DNA polymerase, resulting in high coverage but exhibiting amplification bias[6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR6 "Dean, F. B., Nelson, J. R., Giesler, T. L. & Lasken, R. S. Rapid amplification of plasmid and phage DNA using Phi 29 DNA polymerase and multiply-primed rolling circle amplification. Genome Res. 11, 1095–1099 (2001)."). However, methods that rely on WGA produce artifacts that complicate the discovery of variants. These artifacts include locus and allelic dropouts, uneven amplification, chimeric DNA molecules, and base-copy errors.

Primary template-directed amplification (PTA) is a recently reported method that builds on MDA to achieve quasilinear amplification. By incorporating exonuclease-resistant terminators, PTA suppresses additional amplification, resulting in higher accuracy, uniformity, and reproducibility than other methods for single-cell genome analysis[7](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR7 "Gonzalez-Pena, V. et al. Accurate genomic variant detection in single cells with primary template-directed amplification. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. USA 118, e2024176118 (2021)."). Multiplexed end-tagging amplification of complementary strands (META-CS) allows the accurate identification of de novo single-nucleotide variants (SNVs) in a single cell. This enables amplification in a one-tube reaction while labeling the two DNA strands differently, facilitating the comparison of complementary positions and filtering out false positives[8](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR8 "Xing, D., Tan, L., Chang, C., Li, H. & Xie, X. S. Accurate SNV detection in single cells by transposon-based whole-genome amplification of complementary strands. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. USA 118, e2013106118 (2021).").

Microfluidic-based WGA methods are promising and are actively being developed. These methods offer numerous advantages over traditional WGA techniques, making them highly attractive for single-cell genomic research. Microfluidic platforms provide a high degree of automation and integration, allowing multiple steps in the WGA process to be performed within a single device. This integration simplifies workflow, saves time, and minimizes the risk of sample contamination or loss[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR3 "Autebert, J. et al. Microfluidic: an innovative tool for efficient cell sorting. Methods 57, 297–307 (2012).").

Single-stranded sequencing using microfluidic reactors (SISSOR)[9](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR9 "Chu, W. K. et al. Ultraaccurate genome sequencing and haplotyping of single human cells. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. USA 114, 12512–12517 (2017).") utilizes a microfluidic device to isolate DNA in a single cell. The DNA is then separated into Watson and Crick strands, which are randomly partitioned into nanoliter-scale compartments within the device. This partitioning allows for the amplification and barcoded library preparation of genomic DNA. SISSOR offers high sequencing accuracy with low error rates, although it may exhibit reduced genomic coverage owing to the potential loss of DNA fragments during the strand separation and partitioning processes.

### Transcriptome

The transcriptome directly influences protein translation. Although the genetic information is nearly identical across all human cells, the transcriptomic data of individual cells reveals distinct gene activity patterns. Recent advances in single-cell RNA-seq have enabled the characterization of cells at the single-cell and spatiotemporal levels and the establishment of diverse projects, such as the Human Cell Atlas project[10](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR10 "Panina, Y., Karagiannis, P., Kurtz, A., Stacey, G. N. & Fujibuchi, W. Human Cell Atlas and cell-type authentication for regenerative medicine. Exp. Mol. Med. 52, 1443–1451 (2020)."). In addition to characterization, scRNA-seq has been used to study cytidine deaminase (CDA) as a potential druggable target in _ALK_ fusion-positive non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC)[11](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR11 "Heo, H. et al. DNA methylome and single-cell transcriptome analyses reveal CDA as a potential druggable target for ALK inhibitor–resistant lung cancer therapy. Exp. Mol. Med. 54, 1236–1249 (2022).").

Cell expression by linear amplification and sequencing2 (CEL-seq2) enhances CEL-seq using barcodes that incorporate UMIs and random priming, leading to improved read mapping percentages and sensitivity while reducing bias[12](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR12 "Hashimshony, T. et al. CEL-Seq2: sensitive highly-multiplexed single-cell RNA-Seq. Genome Biol. 17, 1–7 (2016)."). In massive parallel single-cell RNA sequencing (MARS-seq2.0), the reaction volume is reduced during reverse transcription, resulting in decreased noise and increased sensitivity[13](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR13 "Keren-Shaul, H. et al. MARS-seq2. 0: an experimental and analytical pipeline for indexed sorting combined with single-cell RNA sequencing. Nat. Protoc. 14, 1841–1862 (2019)."). However, these methods primarily capture 3′ end transcripts, making it difficult to determine sequences or isoforms at the 5′ end[13](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR13 "Keren-Shaul, H. et al. MARS-seq2. 0: an experimental and analytical pipeline for indexed sorting combined with single-cell RNA sequencing. Nat. Protoc. 14, 1841–1862 (2019).").

Droplet-based technologies such as 10X Genomics Chromium[14](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR14 "Zheng, G. X. et al. Massively parallel digital transcriptional profiling of single cells. Nat. Commun. 8, 14049 (2017).") and Drop-seq[15](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR15 "Macosko, E. Z. et al. Highly parallel genome-wide expression profiling of individual cells using nanoliter droplets. Cell 161, 1202–1214 (2015).") have become popular owing to their cost-effectiveness and high throughput. Beads are used to capture RNA from the oil and create reaction droplets. Chromium uses soft hydrogel beads, whereas Drop-seq uses small hard resin beads, resulting in different cell capture rates.

Split pool ligation-based transcriptome sequencing (SPLiT-seq) involves iterative splitting and pooling of cells, allowing for diverse cell barcode combinations[16](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR16 "Rosenberg, A. B. et al. Single-cell profiling of the developing mouse brain and spinal cord with split-pool barcoding. Science 360, 176–182 (2018)."). This method accommodates fixed cells or nuclei and offers flexibility in experimental design[16](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR16 "Rosenberg, A. B. et al. Single-cell profiling of the developing mouse brain and spinal cord with split-pool barcoding. Science 360, 176–182 (2018).").

Several methods, including molecular crowding single-cell RNA barcoding and sequencing (mcSCRB-seq)[17](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR17 "Bagnoli, J. W. et al. Sensitive and powerful single-cell RNA sequencing using mcSCRB-seq. Nat. Commun. 9, 2937 (2018)."), switching mechanism at 5′ end of RNA template sequencing3 (SMART-seq3)[18](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR18 "Hagemann-Jensen, M. et al. Single-cell RNA counting at allele and isoform resolution using Smart-seq3. Nat. Biotechnol. 38, 708–714 (2020)."), and FLASH-seq[19](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR19 "Hahaut, V. et al. Fast and highly sensitive full-length single-cell RNA sequencing using FLASH-seq. Nat. Biotechnol. 40, 1447–1451 (2022)."), employ full-length cDNA library construction and sequencing. These methods utilize template-switching oligos (TSOs) to create full-length cDNA libraries and identify the 5′ ends of transcripts, and they incorporate UMIs to mitigate PCR bias. FLASH-seq combines split reverse transcription and PCR processes with an improved reverse transcriptase, enhancing cDNA yield and reducing amplification noise and hands-on time[19](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR19 "Hahaut, V. et al. Fast and highly sensitive full-length single-cell RNA sequencing using FLASH-seq. Nat. Biotechnol. 40, 1447–1451 (2022).").

Vast transcriptome analyses of single cells using dA-tailing (VASA-seq)[20](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR20 "Salmen, F. et al. High-throughput total RNA sequencing in single cells using VASA-seq. Nat. Biotechnol. 40, 1780–1793 (2022).") are available in both plate-based and droplet-based formats, providing versatility based on experimental objectives. This approach enables the formation of full-length cDNA libraries and the capture of nonpolyadenylated transcripts. VASA-seq can detect coding RNAs, long noncoding RNAs, transcription factors, and small noncoding RNAs[20](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR20 "Salmen, F. et al. High-throughput total RNA sequencing in single cells using VASA-seq. Nat. Biotechnol. 40, 1780–1793 (2022).").

All of the abovementioned scRNA-seq methods have constraints in capturing longer transcripts, identifying splicing events, and distinguishing between transcript isoforms because they are based on short-read sequencing. Specialized methods have been developed to address these limitations. Multiplexed array isoform sequencing (MAS-ISO-seq) employs long-read sequencing by attaching a dU-containing adapter to cDNA ends, enabling the sequencing of long reads in a single pass[21](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR21 "Al’Khafaji, A. M. et al. High-throughput RNA isoform sequencing using programmable cDNA concatenation. Biorxiv: 2021.10. 01.462818 (2021)."). Single-nucleus isoform RNA sequencing (SnISOr-seq) distinguishes intronic reads by splitting transcripts into intronic and exonic cDNAs and analyzing only exonic cDNA through long-read sequencing, thereby enhancing the analysis of the desired exons[22](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR22 "Hardwick, S. A. et al. Single-nuclei isoform RNA sequencing unlocks barcoded exon connectivity in frozen brain tissue. Nat. Biotechnol. 40, 1082–1092 (2022)."). Long-read sequencing-based methods such as MAS-ISO-seq and SnISOr-seq address the limitations of short-read sequencing-based methods and offer improved capabilities for characterizing longer transcripts and transcript isoforms[23](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR23 "Zhang, Y., Lu, L. & Li, X. Detection technologies for RNA modifications. Exp. Mol. Med. 54, 1601–1616 (2022)."). The properties of these methods are listed in Table [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#Tab1).

### Proteome

Proteome research at the single-cell level provides a wealth of information about the diversity of proteins within a cell population and important insights into cellular functions, disease mechanisms, and developmental processes because proteins can be considered the end material of biological processes in cells.

Mass spectrometry (MS) allows researchers to identify and quantify proteins based on their mass-to-charge ratios (m/z). MS-based methods can be used for single-cell protein sequencing by coupling with other techniques to capture and isolate individual cells.

Matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization (MALDI)[24](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR24 "Nadler, W. M. et al. MALDI versus ESI: the impact of the ion source on peptide identification. J. Proteome Res. 16, 1207–1215 (2017).") and laser ablation electrospray ionization (LAESI) are MS-based techniques commonly used for single-cell protein sequencing. In MALDI, a laser is used to ionize proteins on a matrix-coated surface, which are then detected using a mass spectrometer. In LAESI, a laser is used to ablate proteins directly from the cell surface, after which the proteins are ionized and detected using a mass spectrometer. These techniques offer high sensitivity and are well suited for analyzing small numbers of cells[24](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR24 "Nadler, W. M. et al. MALDI versus ESI: the impact of the ion source on peptide identification. J. Proteome Res. 16, 1207–1215 (2017).").

Fluorescence-based methods are another approach to single-cell protein sequencing. These methods rely on fluorescent probes or antibodies that specifically bind to the proteins of interest. The fluorescence signal is detected and quantified using microscopy or flow cytometry. Single-cell western blotting (scWB)[25](https://www.nature.com/articles/s12276-024-01186-2#ref-CR25 "Hughes, A. # The technological landscape and applications of single-cell multi-omics

### Subjects

## Abstract

Single-cell multi-omics technologies and methods characterize cell states and activities by simultaneously integrating various single-modality omics methods that profile the transcriptome, genome, epigenome, epitranscriptome, proteome, metabolome and other (emerging) omics. Collectively, these methods are revolutionizing molecular cell biology research. In this comprehensive Review, we discuss established multi-omics technologies as well as cutting-edge and state-of-the-art methods in the field. We discuss how multi-omics technologies have been adapted and improved over the past decade using a framework characterized by optimization of throughput and resolution, modality integration, uniqueness and accuracy, and we also discuss multi-omics limitations. We highlight the impact that single-cell multi-omics technologies have had in cell lineage tracing, tissue-specific and cell-specific atlas production, tumour immunology and cancer genetics, and in mapping of cellular spatial information in fundamental and translational research. Finally, we discuss bioinformatics tools that have been developed to link different omics modalities and elucidate functionality through the use of better mathematical modelling and computational methods.

Abstract
--------

Over the past decade, with the development of high-throughput single-cell sequencing technology, single-cell omics has been emerged as a powerful tool to understand the molecular basis of cellular mechanisms and refine our knowledge of diverse cell states. They can reveal the heterogeneity at different genetic layers and elucidate their associations by multiple omics analysis, providing a more comprehensive genetic map of biological regulatory networks. In the post-GWAS era, the molecular biological mechanisms influencing human diseases will be further elucidated by single-cell omics. This review mainly summarizes the development and trend of single-cell omics. This involves single-cell omics technologies, single-cell multi-omics technologies, multiple omics data integration methods, applications in various human organs and diseases, classic laboratory cell lines, and animal disease models. The review will reveal some perspectives for elucidating human diseases and constructing animal models.

**Keywords:** single-cell omics, multi-omics integration, human disease, cell lines, animal disease models

1 Introduction
--------------

### 1.1 The introduction of single-cell omics

Cells are the structural and functional units of organisms. Traditional biological research is mostly at the population level, focusing on the average of the tissue/cell population but ignoring the characteristics of individual cells. Cells are heterogeneous, not only are there differences in cell phenotypes but also in their biological functions, such as transcriptional regulation, gene expression, and signal transduction ([Wen and Tang, 2018a](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B178); [Chappell et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B18)). For example, the heterogeneity among malignant tumor cells is of great significance for analyzing the mutation mode, regional differences, evolutionary laws, and drug resistance mechanisms ([Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#F1)). Following the considerable development of single cell isolation and next-generation sequencing (NGS) ([Mardis, 2011](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B111)), single-cell transcriptome ([Tang et al., 2009](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B164)), genome ([Navin et al., 2011](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B125)), and methylation sequencing ([Guo et al., 2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B57)) have emerged. A state of the cell is determined by the dynamic states of its three-dimensional (3D) chromatin conformation, chromatin remodeling, regulatory element interaction, epigenetic modification, transcriptional regulation, gene expression, proteome, and metabolome ([Chappell et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B18)). These have also become the basis for single-cell research, revealing the diversity of cell types and subsets ([Figure 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#F2)). Simultaneously, a series of single-cell mono-omic sequencing technologies were developed for the purpose.

Traditional biological research is carried out from bulk cell levels, and the experimental results are often the averaged gene expression of the cell population, or only represent the life activity information of the dominant cell in the number and cannot accurately reflect a lot of information about cell heterogeneity in the sample. It ignores the differences in gene expression regulation between different population and single cells. Single-cell sequencing, which is sequenced at the individual cell level, solves the heterogeneity of genetic variation between different cells.

These technologies reveal the heterogeneity of single cells on different levels. But each cell can only be used to reveal one kind of omic information. The main challenge in current single-cell omics is determining how to stimunously reveal various omic features within an individual cell. Until recently, single-cell multi-omics has been an effective solution. On the one hand, single-cell multi-omic intergrating analysis can combine many single-cell mono-omic sequencing data to elucidate life activities. On the other hand, they are able to obtain two or more types of omic data from one cell at the same time.

### 1.2 Development of single-cell omics

Single-cell transcriptomics can intuitively reflect heterogeneity and functional differences in expression levels and is often used as a referenceable single-cell omics technique ([Efremova and Teichmann, 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B36)). In addition, molecular cellular identity is a product of the interplay between various modalities. It is essential to make coordinated measurements linking different regulatory layers to comprehensively understand how individual cells can demonstrate heterogeneity ([Ogbeide et al., 2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B128)). Parallel to the rapid and widespread adoption of transcriptomics, other single-cell omics technologies have been developed and evolved in a variety aspects within cells, including the genome ([Gawad et al., 2016](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B47); [Lan et al., 2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B90)), methylome ([Guo et al., 2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B57); [Guo et al., 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B58); [Guo et al., 2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B56); [Schwartzman and Tanay, 2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B149)), histone modification ([Park, 2009](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B132); [Rotem et al., 2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B146)), chromatin accessibility ([Buenrostro et al., 2015a](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B11); [Chen et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B21)), chromatin conformation ([Nagano et al., 2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B119); [Nagano et al., 2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B121); [Nagano et al., 2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B120)), proteome ([Shahi et al., 2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B150)), nucleosome localization ([Chereji et al., 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B23)), spatial transcriptome ([Ståhl et al., 2016](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B156)), metagenomic ([Yilmaz and Singh, 2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B186)) and even microbiome ([Lloréns-Rico et al., 2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B104)). Absorbingly, the development of single-cell omics has laid the foundation for capturing multiple omics in a single cell.

In addition to the multi-omics joint analysis of single cell omics, single-cell multi-omics has also developed into some high-order techniques such as single-cell two-omics and single-cell triomics in recent years. Single-cell multi-omics (≥2) technologies sequence and analyze multiple omics for single cells. In 2015, G&T-seq (genome and transcriptome sequencing) and DR-seq (gDNA-mRNA sequencing) have been developed for parallel measuring genomic and transcriptomic data, but the throughput is low ([Dey et al., 2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B34); [Macaulay et al., 2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B109)). Excitingly, with the advent of 10×Genomics strategy in 2016, single-cell multi-omics technology is advancing rapidly. Following single-cell sequencing technology, which was named Nature’s 2013 Technology of the Year ([Nature Methods, 2013)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B200), single-cell multi-omics became the Technology of the Year in 2019 ([Nature Methods, 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B4)).

### 1.3 Studying single-cell omics for function genetic research in human and animal model

Over the past decade, genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have uncovered thousands of genes and genetic variants (SNPs) associated with human disease ([Boyle et al., 2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B9)). However, the biological mechanisms and functional features behind these associations have not yet been adequately mined ([Klein et al., 2005](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B80); [Gallagher and Chen-Plotkin, 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B45)). Most SNP loci are found in non-coding regions of the genome by whole genome sequencing and resequencing, which complicates understanding the pathogenic mechanisms of SNPs. Additionally, the transcriptome, metabolome, proteome, and phenome also provide rich information about phenotypic variation. Integrating GWAS data and phenotypic variation is an important step in studying pathogenesis. Generally, a large number of SNPs play roles at the tissue or cell-specific level. Due to the cellular heterogeneity of the focal organs, single cell resolution research is needed. Using the single-cell omic methodology, scientists could map disease-associated SNPs or genes to cell types at the single-cell level, reveal the heterogeneity among cells at multiple omic levels and elucidate their different omic associations or variations, which would provide new directions for functional genetic research in human diseases and animal models ([Timshel et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B166); [Tomaszewski et al., 2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B167)).

Although there are many excellent reviews about single-cell sequencing ([Nawy, 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B126); [Schwartzman and Tanay, 2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B149); [Wang and Navin, 2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B176); [Bian et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B7); [Wen and Tang, 2018b](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B179); [Kang et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B76); [Tang et al., 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B165); [Zhu C. et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B195); [He et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B65); [Lee et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B92); [Lloréns-Rico et al., 2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B104)), we review the methodological progress and application of single-cell omics and single-cell multiomics, computational methods and tools for single-cell omics analysis, and the future research trend for analyzing the pathogenesis of human diseases and animal models in the post-GWAS era. The review tries to reveal some new ideas in analyzing disease mechanisms and constructing animal models of human diseases.

2 The methodological progress of single-cell omics and single-cell multi-omics
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.1 Single cell isolation

Efficient isolation of single cells is a prerequisite for single-cell omics and multi-omics techniques. The quality of separation technology is mainly determined by its integrity, purity, quality, sensitivity, and throughput ([Gross et al., 2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B53)). Based on these requirements, a variety of different isolation methods have been developed, such as manual cell picking, limiting dilution, laser cell manipulation (LCM), microfluidics, fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS), and magnetic-activated cell sorting (MACS). They are illuminated in [Figure 3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#F3).

Single-cell isolation techniques. The principles of each technique are described in the text. Abbreviation: LCM, laser capture microdissection; FACS, fluorescence-activated cell sorting; MACS, magnetic-activated cell sorting ([Gross et al., 2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B53); [Hu et al., 2016](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B70)).

Particularly, the application of cell barcoding with combinatorial indexing has gradually emerged in recent years. Cell barcoding is a single-cell tracking strategy, that is, capable of sequencing libraries from multiple cells ([Cusanovich et al., 2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B31)). FACS can use the combined index strategy to classify the cells in the plates using a unique barcode marker, so that each cell has a unique barcode to achieve the purpose of single cell separation. But combinatorial indexing methods can lead to the loss of many cells ([Chappell et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B18)). Except for the methods mentioned in [Figure 3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#F3), other single-cell isolation techniques are comprehensively summarized in [Supplementary Table S1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#s9).

Due to the technical limitations of preparing suspensions directly from fresh tissue to isolate intact single cells, it is challenging for researchers to analyze gene expression at the single-cell level. Interestingly, single-nucleus library construction sequencing technology was invented. For instance, sNuc-seq is capable of isolating a single nucleus, using it as the raw material, and resolving different cell types and dynamic changes ([Grindberg et al., 2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B52)).

### 2.2 Single-cell omics technologies

Here we briefly introduce the use and methodological development of multiplex single-cell mono-omics sequencing technologies.

#### 2.2.1 Single-cell genomics

The research of single-cell genetic information dates back to the 1970s in the field of cell and immunology, where cytologists used karyotyping, fluorescence _in situ_ hybridization (FISH) and Giemsa staining to study genome rearrangement at the single-cell level. With the advent of PCR technology in the 1980s, scientists were able to directly amplify and sequence single-cell genomic DNA, but with limited coverage of the genome. The isolation of Phi29 (Φ29) and Bst polymerase brought single-cell genome research into the era of whole genome amplification (WGA) ([Navin and Hicks, 2011](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B124)). WGA combined with the probe-based array-based Comparative Genomic Hybridization (aCGH) method could detect single-cell genome copy number variation, but with low resolution and reproducibility, even high noise ([Fiegler et al., 2007](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B41); [Navin et al., 2011](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B125)). Differs from aCGH technology, the NGS detects whole genome DNA in a continuous, long, non-targeted manner with the improved throughput and lower costs, has become a priority choice ([Navin and Hicks, 2011](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B124)). Combining WGA and NGS, single-cell whole genome sequencing (scWGS) can effectively reveal genetic variation in the genome of a single cell, such as copy number variation (CNV), single-nucleotide variants (SNV), and other structural variants ([Navin et al., 2011](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B125)). A variety of single-cell genome sequencing technologies have become powerful tools for detecting cellular heterogeneity, such as single-cell genomic sequencing (SIC-seq), single-cell combinatorial marker sequencing (SCI-seq), and topographic single cell sequencing (TSCS) ([Navin et al., 2011](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B125); [Vitak et al., 2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B170); [Casasent et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B17)). However, due to the short read limit of the second-generation sequencing platform, these methods cannot exhaustively detect genomic structural variation (including insertion, deletion, repetition, translocation, etc.) ([Futreal et al., 2004](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B44)). Interestingly, cytogenetic profiles are also of importance as the karyotype alterations (both structural and numerical) change the system information by creating new karyotype coding ([Ye et al., 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B185)). Recently genome chaos, including chromothripsis, polyploidy giant cancer cells (PGCCs), and micronuclei clusters, has become a hot topic ([Stephens et al., 2011](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B157); [Liu et al., 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B98); [Niu et al., 2016](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B127)), as they are essential for genome instability-mediated somatic evolution. Single-cell omics are needed to characterize these reorganized karyotypes. Methods are also required to convert the sequence data into cytogenetic data ([Zhang and Kschischo, 2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846559/#B191)). Tang et al. recently developed a single-cell genome sequencing method based on the third-generation sequencing (TGS) platform, called SMOOTH-seq (single-molecule real-time sequencing of long fragments amplified through transposon insertion). Recent advancements in single-cell RNA sequencing, spatial transcriptomics, and multi-omics integration have enabled researchers to dissect complex tissues and identify distinct cell populations with unique functional states. This review discusses the application of single-cell omics in diverse fields, including cancer research and developmental biology, showcasing how they reveal insights into cellular interactions, disease mechanisms, and therapeutic responses. Notable studies illustrate the potential of single-cell approaches to uncover novel biomarkers and therapeutic targets, particularly in heterogeneous diseases such as cancer and neurodevelopmental disorders. Furthermore, the review emphasizes the importance of integrating single-cell data with computational models to enhance our understanding of cellular dynamics and microenvironmental influences. Overall, this review underscores the critical role of single-cell omics in advancing our knowledge of biology and its applications in clinical settings, paving the way for personalized medicine.

Introduction
------------

Understanding cellular heterogeneity, the diversity in cell types, states, and functions within a biological system is fundamental to advancing biology and medicine. Single-cell omics emerged as a cutting-edge technique in molecular biology that combines multiple types of omics data types, such as genomics, transcriptomics, epigenomics, and proteomics from individual cells. Unlike traditional bulk methods, which average measurements across large cell populations, single-cell omics capture the heterogeneity within individual cells, providing a detailed view of cellular states, functions, and interactions ([Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#fig1)a) [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib1)]. For example, in cancer research, single-cell omics has revolutionized cancer research by providing unprecedented insights into tumor biology, heterogeneity, microenvironment interactions, and the mechanisms driving cancer progression, metastasis, and therapy resistance [[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib2)]. Developmental biology also benefits, with trajectory tools tracing lineage progression and regulatory shifts during differentiation [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib3)].

Figure 1. **Overview of Single-cell OMICS techniques and their applications in basic and clinical research. a)** Single-cell multiomics technique overview and its application in research and clinic. **b)** Single-cell Omics applications in scientific research including Cell heterogeneity, Gene regulatory network, Single-cell Perturbation, Cell lineage, Cell–cell interactions and Drug repurposing. **c)** Application of single-cell omics methods in Diagnostics, Prognostics, and Precision medicine clinical applications.

Beyond basic research, single-cell approaches are transforming clinical science [[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib4)]. For instance, single-cell omics has been used to study breast cancer [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib5)], autoimmune diseases [[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib6)], neurological disorders [[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib7)], developmental disorders [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib8)], cardiovascular diseases [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib9)] and infectious diseases [[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib10)], revealing insights into cell subpopulations, immune cell interactions, and disease-specific gene expression changes. These advancements provide a comprehensive view of cellular processes at unprecedented resolution, paving the way for personalized medicine and targeted therapies.

This review explores single-cell omics techniques ([Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#fig1)a), and their utility in basic and translational research ([Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#fig1)b), such as _decoding cellular heterogeneity, reconstructing gene regulatory networks, single-cell functional analysis through perturbation, cell lineage trajectory reconstruction, cell–cell communication, drug repurposing_, and success stories in clinical trials ([Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#fig1)c). These advancements are driving discoveries and inspiring new therapies, bridging basic research and clinical innovation.

Combining single-cell omics techniques for basic and translational research
---------------------------------------------------------------------------

The combination of scRNA-seq with other techniques is revolutionizing basic and translational research. For example, the single-cell multiome technique from 10X Genomics, can jointly profile gene expression (scRNA-seq) as well as chromatin accessibility (scATAC-seq) from the same single-cell ([Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#tbl1)). Similarly, single-cell SNMT-seq is a multi-omics sequencing technique that allows simultaneous profiling of three critical layers of gene regulation i.e. chromatin accessibility, DNA methylation, and gene expression at the single-cell level. Similarly, several other techniques have been developed combining multiomics profiles to provide much deeper insights into the molecular changes at single-cell resolution ([Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#tbl1)). Furthermore, recent advancements in spatial technologies enable the integration of single-cell resolution with spatial context, allowing researchers to analyze multiple molecular layers—including the genome, transcriptome, proteome, and epigenome within their native tissue environment. For instance, NanoString's GeoMx DSP has been instrumental in uncovering inter- and intra-tumoral heterogeneity, mapping the spatial organization of tumors into distinct cellular communities, and identifying treatment-induced remodelling. In pancreatic cancer, this technology has provided clinically relevant prognostic insights, paving the way for more precise therapeutic strategies [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib11)]. Similarly, a recent study utilized the Xenium platform [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib12)]∗∗, coupled with single-cell analyses, providing a deeper understanding of tumor heterogeneity and treatment response in brain cancer [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib13)]. Though these methods offer advanced spatial transcriptomics capabilities, it comes with several limitations such as limited resolution, low transcript coverage, tissue preparation challenges, limited multi-Omics integration, higher cost, and high computational requirements [[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib14)].

Table 1. Single-cell MultiOmics techniques.

| 2 | DR-seq | Simultaneously sequences DNA and RNA from the same single cell. |

| 4 | SNARE-seq | Profiles chromatin accessibility (via ATAC-seq) and RNA-seq simultaneously. |

Over the past decade, the routine application of scRNA-seq on large-scale cellular populations has profoundly advanced our understanding of the inherent heterogeneity within complex biological systems. Meanwhile, independent breakthroughs in mass spectrometry (MS) technologies have brought us closer to achieving high-resolution single-cell proteome characterization, further bridging the gap between transcriptomics and proteomics at the single-cell level. Several MS techniques such as SCoPE-MS (Single-Cell Proteomics by Mass Spectrometry) [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib15)], SCoPE2 [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib16)], and nanoPOTS (nanodroplet processing in one-pot) [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib17)], have been developed for scientific research. Similarly, antibody-based approaches such as CyTOF (Mass Cytometry) and imaging-based proteomics techniques like Imaging Mass Cytometry (IMC) have enabled the high-dimensional visualization of normal and diseased tissues in situ.

The integration of these cutting-edge technologies hold immense potential in exploring immune cell characterization, developmental biology, and disease pathology, offering unprecedented insights into cellular mechanisms. By capturing intricate molecular and spatial interactions, these approaches are reshaping our understanding of complex biological systems and paving the way for innovative therapeutic strategies [[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib18)].∗.

Unraveling cellular heterogeneity with single-cell omics
--------------------------------------------------------

Cellular heterogeneity refers to the diversity of cell types, states, and functions within a tissue, organ, or biological system—an essential characteristic of multicellular organisms ([Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#fig2)a) [[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib19)]. Understanding this diversity is crucial for deciphering complex biological processes, including development, immune responses, and disease progression. Single-cell omics technologies enable the identification of previously unknown cell types, developmental trajectories, and cell–state transitions, offering transformative insights into cellular biology. For instance, a study employing single-cell multiomics (CITE-seq) profiled leukemic stem cells (LSCs) and progenitors, revealing their dynamic molecular heterogeneity and its role in variable therapeutic responses [[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib20)]∗∗. Similarly, spatial and single-cell transcriptomic profiling of the lymph node microenvironment in intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma uncovered key mechanisms driving metastasis and tissue remodeling [[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib21)].

Recent innovations, such as Dyna-vivo-seq, introduced by Yin et al. (2024), utilize in vivo metabolic RNA labeling to track cellular RNA dynamics during acute kidney injury (AKI). This scRNA-seq approach provides a powerful tool for studying temporal transcription dynamics at a single-cell resolution, offering key insights into cellular responses and gene expression changes over time [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib22)].

Furthermore, integrative analysis of spatial and single-cell transcriptomics has provided a high-resolution view of papillary thyroid cancer (PTC) evolution, shedding light on tumor heterogeneity, microenvironment interactions, and molecular progression. These findings hold significant promise for developing targeted therapeutic strategies [[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib23)].

The application of single-cell omics and spatial profiling continues to revolutionize our understanding of cellular complexity, driving novel discoveries across developmental biology, immunology, and cancer research.

Advances in gene regulatory network analysis using single-cell omics
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Gene regulatory networks (GRNs) function as cellular control systems, where regulatory proteins such as transcription factors and genomic elements interact to modulate gene expression, ultimately shaping cellular function and development. Understanding how GRNs orchestrate biological processes and maintain cell states is fundamental to uncovering the mechanisms driving health and disease ([Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#fig2)b). The advent of single-cell multi-omics technologies has revolutionized GRN reconstruction, enabling higher-resolution insights into gene regulation across diverse biological and disease contexts.

Among recent innovations, Dictys [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib24)]∗, a dynamic GRN inference framework, integrates chromatin accessibility and gene expression data to reconstruct time-resolved and cell-type-specific GRNs. By leveraging transcription factor footprinting and stochastic modeling, Dictys has been successfully applied in developmental studies of human blood and mouse skin, offering deeper insights into dynamic gene regulation. Similarly, FigR [[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib25)] connects distal cis-regulatory elements with their target genes, enabling functional GRN inference. By combining scRNA-seq and scATAC-seq data, FigR identified Domains of Regulatory Chromatin (DORCs), revealing key regulatory regions that drive immune responses.

Single-cell GRN analysis has also provided groundbreaking insights into neurological disorders. Wamsley et al. [[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib26)]∗∗ utilized single-nucleus RNA and ATAC sequencing to dissect GRNs in autism spectrum disorder (ASD), identifying cell-type-specific regulatory networks influenced by both rare and common genetic variants. Their findings linked GRN dysregulation to cellular and circuit-level abnormalities in the autistic brain.

Despite these advances, scaling GRN inference methods to accommodate increasingly complex datasets remains a challenge. As single-cell and multi-omics technologies continue to evolve, further computational and experimental innovations will be essential to unlock the full potential of GRN-based discoveries in developmental biology, immunology, and disease research.

Functional perturbations at the single-cell level
-------------------------------------------------

Single-cell perturbation techniques integrate experimental interventions (e.g., CRISPR-based gene editing, chemical treatments) with single-cell resolution measurements (e.g., transcriptomics, epigenomics, proteomics) to study how specific perturbations influence cellular states and functions ([Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#tbl1)). These methods serve as powerful tools for dissecting gene regulatory networks (GRNs), identifying drug targets, and understanding cellular responses to environmental changes.

For instance, CROP-seq [[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib27)] enables the simultaneous study of genetic perturbations and their transcriptional effects in thousands of individual cells, while DRUG-seq [[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib28)] facilitates detailed transcriptional profiling of drug responses, accelerating drug discovery efforts.

Given the cost and labor-intensive nature of experimental perturbation studies, computational approaches—termed in silico perturbation methods—have emerged. These leverage single-cell multiomics data to model gene expression changes following virtual perturbations of candidate genes and providing deeper insights into gene regulation and cellular dynamics ([Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#fig2)c).

Notable examples include:
*   •CellOracle [[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib29)], which reconstructs GRNs and simulates transcription factor perturbations using single-cell data. 
*   •Amrute et al. (2023) [[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib30)], who used in silico RUNX1 perturbation to predict cardiac recovery, simulating knockout effects on macrophages and fibroblasts, later validated in vivo. 
*   •Quan et al. (2024) [[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib31)], who applied in silico gene perturbation to uncover type-I interferon regulatory mechanisms in Parkinson's disease, identifying NFATC2 as a key regulator of neuroinflammation. 
*   •Yan et al. (2024) [[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib32)], who conducted in silico perturbation of SHOX2 and MEOX2, revealing their essential roles in anterior and posterior palate development, providing insights into palatogenesis and cleft palate formation. 

Together, these studies highlight the growing impact of single-cell perturbation techniques, both experimental and computational, in dissecting disease mechanisms, investigating cellular responses, and identifying novel therapeutic targets. As these approaches continue to evolve, they promise to further redefine our understanding of gene function and regulation at the single-cell level.

Lineage trajectory analysis through single-cell omics
-----------------------------------------------------

Lineage trajectory analysis through single-cell omics has revolutionized our ability to dissect cellular trajectories and dynamic processes, enabling researchers to profile molecular changes at the single-cell level and gain deeper insights into cellular states, differentiation pathways, and molecular mechanisms underlying health and disease ([Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#fig2)d). Trajectory analysis in scRNA-seq data models the progression of cells through continuous biological processes, such as differentiation, by ordering them along a pseudo-time axis based on their gene expression profiles. Combining trajectory inference with RNA velocity further enhances our ability to reconstruct differentiation pathways, while RNA velocity itself models cellular gene expression dynamics by analyzing unspliced and spliced mRNA [[33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0955067425000869#bib33)]∗∗.

Deep learning, a subset of artificial intelligence in which computational models learn to recognize patterns and make predictions by processing large datasets through artificial neural networks, is increasingly being incorporated into single-cell omics analysis. ## Abstract

Single-cell sequencing methods provide the highest resolution insight into cellular heterogeneity. Owing to their rapid growth and decreasing cost, they are now widely accessible to scientists worldwide. Single-cell technologies enable analysis of a large number of cells, making them powerful tools to characterise rare cell types and refine our understanding of diverse cell states. Moreover, single-cell application in biomedical sciences helps to unravel mechanisms related to disease pathogenesis and outcome. In this Viewpoint, we briefly describe existing single-cell methods (genomics, transcriptomics, epigenomics, proteomics, and mulitomics), comment on available analysis tools, and give examples of method applications in the biomedical field.

**Keywords:**
bioinformatics; epigenomics; genomics; haematology; multiomics; proteomics; single-cell methods; transcriptomics.
